name: Mzansi Mix Vol. 2
description: South African daily life, taxi culture, braai traditions, load shedding facts, and everyday Mzansi knowledge
author: Game Night Crew

questions.question: What is the most popular meat grilled at a traditional South African braai?
questions.explanation: Boerewors is the undisputed king of the braai! No South African braai is complete without a coil of wors sizzling on the grid. Lekker, ne?

questions.question: What does the SA slang word 'eish' generally express?
questions.explanation: Eish! It's one of those uniquely South African words that covers everything from shock to frustration to disbelief. You know it when you feel it!

questions.question: Bongani has 3 sisters and each sister has 1 brother. How many children are in the family?
questions.explanation: Bongani IS the one brother that each sister has! So it's 3 sisters + 1 brother (Bongani) = 4 kids. Don't let this one catch you!

questions.question: In a minibus taxi, what does the hand signal of pointing your index finger upward usually mean?
questions.explanation: One finger up means you're heading to town, the CBD, or the main rank. Every taxi commuter in Mzansi knows this one — it's taxi sign language 101!

questions.question: If you're facing north and turn 180 degrees, which direction are you now facing?
questions.explanation: 180 degrees is a half turn, so you go from north straight to south. Easy peasy!

questions.question: Which South African city is famous for being the birthplace of bunny chow?
questions.explanation: Durban is the home of the bunny chow! This iconic hollowed-out bread loaf filled with curry was created by the Indian community in Durbs. A must-try for any visitor!

questions.question: What is the name of the loyalty card used by most minibus taxi commuters in Gauteng?
options: There is no standard loyalty card for taxis
questions.explanation: Minibus taxis in SA are still largely a cash-only system with no standard loyalty card. You pay the gaatjie (fare collector) with cash, my friend! Some digital payment pilots have started, but nothing widespread yet.

questions.question: What type of wood is traditionally considered the BEST for a braai in South Africa?
questions.explanation: Sekelbos (sickle bush) is the ultimate braai wood! It burns hot, lasts long, and gives food that amazing smoky flavour. True braai masters know this is the good stuff!

questions.question: What does the SA slang word 'lekker' mean?
questions.explanation: Lekker means nice, great, cool, delicious — basically anything positive! 'That braai was lekker!' is the highest compliment you can give in Mzansi.

questions.question: Sipho is 5th in line at Home Affairs. 3 people leave and 2 more join behind him. What position is he now?
questions.explanation: If 3 people ahead of Sipho leave, he moves from 5th to 2nd. The people joining behind don't affect his position. Still a long wait at Home Affairs though!

questions.question: What is 'load shedding' in South Africa?
options: Rolling blackouts to prevent grid collapse
A type of truck delivery service
Reducing data usage on your phone
questions.explanation: Load shedding is Eskom's way of doing planned rolling blackouts to keep the national power grid from collapsing entirely. South Africans know this struggle all too well — candles, inverters, and cold showers!

questions.question: Which province is Soweto located in?
questions.explanation: Soweto (South Western Townships) is in Gauteng province, south-west of Johannesburg. It's one of the most historically significant places in SA and home to legends like Mandela and Tutu!

questions.question: What is the role of the 'gaatjie' in a minibus taxi?
options: The driver's assistant who collects fares and calls for passengers
The traffic officer at the rank
questions.explanation: The gaatjie is the taxi conductor who hangs out the door, calls out the destination, collects fares, and squeezes passengers in. They're the multitasking heroes of SA public transport!

questions.question: What is biltong made from?
questions.explanation: Biltong is dried, cured meat — usually beef, but also game like kudu or springbok. It's South Africa's favourite snack, and way better than American jerky, just saying!

questions.question: Put these South African cities in order from NORTH to SOUTH: (1) Cape Town, (2) Durban, (3) Polokwane, (4) Johannesburg
questions.explanation: From north to south: Polokwane (Limpopo), Johannesburg (Gauteng), Durban (KZN), Cape Town (Western Cape). Know your geography, boet!

questions.question: Load shedding is on a 4-day cycle. If your area has load shedding at 18:00 today (Monday), on which day will it next be at 18:00?
questions.explanation: A 4-day cycle means the same time slot repeats every 4 days. Monday + 4 days = Friday. Now you can plan your braai!

questions.question: What is a 'spaza shop' in South Africa?
options: A chain of fast food restaurants
questions.explanation: A spaza shop is an informal convenience store usually run from someone's home in a township. They sell everything from bread and milk to airtime and sweets. The backbone of township commerce!

questions.question: Which two football clubs contest the famous 'Soweto Derby'?
questions.explanation: The Soweto Derby between Orlando Pirates (the Buccaneers) and Kaizer Chiefs (Amakhosi) is the biggest match in South African football. The atmosphere is absolutely electric!

questions.question: What is a 'potjiekos'?
options: A type of South African beer
A stew slow-cooked in a cast-iron pot over coals
questions.explanation: Potjiekos is a traditional SA stew cooked slowly in a three-legged cast-iron pot (potjie) over hot coals. The secret? You never stir it — you let the layers cook together. Patience is key!

questions.question: What does it mean when someone says 'just now' in South Africa?
questions.explanation: In SA, 'just now' means sometime in the near future — could be 5 minutes, could be 2 hours! It drives visitors mad. 'Now now' is slightly more urgent, and 'now' actually means now. Confusing, ne?

questions.question: Which South African musician is known as the 'Queen of African Pop' and sang 'Pata Pata'?
questions.explanation: Mama Africa herself — Miriam Makeba! Her hit 'Pata Pata' became a worldwide sensation. She also used her platform to fight against apartheid. A true legend of Mzansi!

questions.question: What is the name of the high-speed rail service connecting Johannesburg, Pretoria, and OR Tambo Airport?
questions.explanation: The Gautrain is Gauteng's rapid rail link connecting Joburg, Pretoria, and OR Tambo International Airport. It's one of the quickest ways to beat the traffic in Jozi!

questions.question: What is a 'kota' (also called a 'skhambane')?
options: A quarter loaf of bread stuffed with chips, cheese, and polony
A type of load shedding schedule
questions.explanation: A kota is a township street food classic — a quarter loaf of white bread hollowed out and stuffed with hot chips, polony, cheese, atchar, and whatever else you fancy. Cheap, filling, and absolutely delicious!

questions.question: How many official languages does South Africa have?
questions.explanation: South Africa has 11 official languages! English, Afrikaans, isiZulu, isiXhosa, Sesotho, Setswana, Sepedi, Xitsonga, siSwati, Tshivenda, and isiNdebele. That's why we're called the Rainbow Nation!

questions.question: What is the traditional South African term for a barbecue?
questions.explanation: It's a braai, not a barbecue! Don't ever call it a BBQ in front of a South African. 'Shisa nyama' (burn the meat) is a township braai spot, but 'braai' is the traditional Afrikaans term that ALL South Africans use.

questions.question: Which famous South African dish consists of spiced minced meat topped with an egg-based custard, originally from Cape Malay cuisine?
questions.explanation: Bobotie is South Africa's national dish! It's a Cape Malay creation of spiced mince baked with an egg custard topping, usually served with yellow rice and chutney. Absolutely divine!

questions.question: What does 'Eskom' stand for?
options: Energy System of Kwazulu and Mpumalanga
questions.explanation: Eskom comes from 'Elektrisiteitsvoorsieningskommissie' — the Afrikaans name for the Electricity Supply Commission. The English abbreviation would be ESCOM, but the Afrikaans name stuck. Now you know!

questions.question: In South African taxi culture, what does it mean when a taxi flashes its hazard lights at another taxi?
options: A greeting or thank you between drivers
questions.explanation: Hazard lights flashed briefly is the taxi driver's way of saying thanks or greeting another driver. It's taxi etiquette! If you see continuous hazards, that's a different story — could mean trouble ahead.

questions.question: What is 'chakalaka'?
options: A spicy vegetable relish often served with pap
questions.explanation: Chakalaka is a spicy vegetable relish made with tomatoes, onions, peppers, carrots, and beans. It's the perfect companion to pap and braai meat. No braai is complete without it!

questions.question: What is the name of the famous horse racing event held annually in Durban, known for its fashion and glamour?
questions.explanation: The Durban July (officially the Hollywoodbets Durban July) is South Africa's biggest and most prestigious horse racing event. It's as much about the fashion and the vibes as the horses!

questions.question: Which South African slang term means to leave or go away quickly?
questions.explanation: A 'sho't left' originally means a quick taxi ride (literally a short left turn), but it's become slang for any quick trip. 'Let me take a sho't left to the shops' is pure Mzansi talk!

questions.question: Three friends — Ayanda, Busi, and Craig — each cooked a different dish: potjie, braai, and bobotie. Ayanda didn't braai. Craig didn't make potjie or braai. What did Ayanda cook?
questions.explanation: Craig didn't make potjie or braai, so Craig made bobotie. Ayanda didn't braai, so Ayanda made potjie. That leaves Busi on the braai. Logic, baby!

questions.question: What is 'pap' (also known as 'mieliepap') made from?
questions.explanation: Pap is made from maize meal (mieliemeel) and is the staple food of South Africa. Whether it's stywepap (stiff), slappap (soft), or krummelpap (crumbly), it goes with everything from wors to chakalaka!

questions.question: Which stage of load shedding is the most severe that South Africa has experienced?
questions.explanation: South Africa experienced up to Stage 6 load shedding during the worst of the Eskom crisis. Each stage means more hours without power. Stage 6 meant up to 6 hours of blackouts per day. Eish, those were dark days — literally!

questions.question: Put these events in the order they happened: (1) 2010 FIFA World Cup in SA, (2) Nelson Mandela released from prison, (3) End of load shedding Stage 6, (4) South Africa became a democracy
questions.explanation: Mandela released (1990), democracy (1994), World Cup (2010), end of Stage 6 load shedding (2024). What a journey, Mzansi!

questions.question: What is the name of the traditional South African dried sausage, similar to biltong but made from cured sausage?
questions.explanation: Droewors (literally 'dry sausage') is a dried sausage snack, thinner than boerewors and cured like biltong. It's the perfect road trip snack alongside your biltong stash!

questions.question: Which South African national park is the largest and most famous for its Big Five wildlife?
questions.explanation: Kruger National Park is nearly 2 million hectares and one of Africa's largest game reserves. It's home to the Big Five (lion, leopard, rhino, elephant, buffalo) and is a bucket-list destination for wildlife lovers worldwide!

questions.question: What are 'amagwinya' (also called 'vetkoek')?
questions.explanation: Amagwinya (vetkoek) are deep-fried dough balls — golden, fluffy, and delicious. Fill them with mince, polony, or even jam and cheese. They're cheap, filling, and you can find them at every taxi rank!

questions.question: What is the significance of Heritage Day (24 September) in South Africa, often called by another name?
questions.explanation: Heritage Day is officially about celebrating SA's diverse cultures, but Jan Scannell (aka Jan Braai) successfully campaigned to also make it National Braai Day. Because what unites ALL South Africans? A braai, of course!

questions.question: Which township is known as the birthplace of kwaito music?
questions.explanation: Kwaito was born in the streets and clubs of Soweto in the early 1990s. Artists like Arthur Mafokate, Trompies, and Boom Shaka put this uniquely South African genre on the map. Kwaito is the sound of freedom!

questions.question: What is 'umqombothi'?
options: A traditional African beer brewed from maize and sorghum
questions.explanation: Umqombothi is a traditional African beer brewed from maize, maize malt, sorghum malt, yeast, and water. It has a thick, gritty texture and is often shared from a communal pot at ceremonies and gatherings. Cheers!

questions.question: The Toyota Quantum is the most common minibus taxi in SA. How many passengers does it officially seat (excluding the driver)?
questions.explanation: The Toyota Quantum officially seats 15 passengers (plus the driver). Although in reality, gaatjies have a magical ability to fit way more than that. 'Inkundla iyashisa!' (there's still space!)

questions.question: What is a 'stoep' in South African culture?
options: A veranda or porch at the front of a house
questions.explanation: A stoep is the veranda or porch at the front of a South African house. It's where you sit with your rooibos tea, watch the neighbourhood, and gossip with the aunties. Every South African home needs a proper stoep!

questions.question: Which South African word means 'look' or 'look at that' and is commonly used to point something out?
questions.explanation: Haibo! (or Hai bo!) is an exclamation of surprise or disbelief, often used when pointing something out. 'Haibo, did you see that taxi overtake on the wrong side of the road?!' Pure Mzansi expression!

questions.question: What is the traditional Afrikaans name for the outdoor fire pit or barbecue structure used for a braai?
questions.explanation: Vuurmaakplek (literally 'fire-making place') is the traditional Afrikaans term for the braai area. Though most people just say 'the braai' these days, real oom knowledge is knowing the old-school terms!

questions.question: Which South African invented the CAT scan (CT scanner)?
questions.explanation: Allan Cormack, born in Johannesburg, co-invented the CT scanner and won the Nobel Prize in Medicine in 1979. South Africa has produced some serious brains! Christiaan Barnard did the first heart transplant — another SA legend.

questions.question: What does the isiZulu word 'indaba' mean, commonly used in South African English?
options: A meeting or conference for discussion
questions.explanation: An indaba is a Zulu word meaning an important meeting or conference. It's been adopted into SA English and is used widely — from business meetings to community gatherings. 'We need to have an indaba about this!'

questions.question: What type of meat are 'skilpadjies' (also called 'muise') made from?
options: Lamb liver wrapped in caul fat (netvet)
questions.explanation: Skilpadjies are lamb's liver wrapped in netvet (caul fat) and grilled on the braai. The name means 'little tortoises' because of how they look! They're a braai delicacy that separates the true braai masters from the amateurs.

questions.question: In which year did South Africa host the FIFA World Cup, featuring the iconic vuvuzela?
questions.explanation: South Africa hosted the 2010 FIFA World Cup — the first ever on African soil! The vuvuzela became world-famous (or infamous, depending on who you ask). That tournament united the nation like nothing else. Ke nako!

questions.question: What is the name of the colourful, beaded Zulu love letter that young women traditionally give to their suitors?
questions.explanation: Ucu is the Zulu beaded love letter. Each colour has a specific meaning — white for purity, red for passion, black for loneliness, blue for faithfulness. A whole love story told through beads! Beautiful tradition.

questions.question: Which South African city was originally called 'eDikeni' by the Xhosa people, later renamed by British settlers, and is now known as a university town in the Eastern Cape?
questions.explanation: Alice, home to the University of Fort Hare, was originally known as eDikeni. The university produced legends like Nelson Mandela, Oliver Tambo, and Robert Mugabe. A small town with a massive legacy!

questions.question: You have 3 identical boxes. One has 2 gold Krugerrands, one has 2 silver coins, one has 1 of each. All labels are WRONG. You pull one coin from the box labelled 'Mixed'. It's gold. What's in the box labelled 'Silver'?
questions.explanation: Since ALL labels are wrong, the 'Mixed' box can't be mixed. You drew gold, so it must be the 2-gold box. The 'Silver' label is also wrong, so it's not silver. The 'Gold' label is wrong, so it's not gold — it must be silver. That leaves 'Silver' box = mixed (1 gold, 1 silver). Big brain time!

questions.question: What is the name of the traditional Xhosa practice where young men undergo initiation (ulwaluko), spending time in the bush?
questions.explanation: Ulwaluko is the Xhosa male initiation ritual marking the transition from boyhood to manhood. Initiates spend weeks in the bush under the guidance of elders. It's a deeply sacred tradition that demands great respect.

questions.question: The Banting diet became hugely popular in South Africa after Professor Tim Noakes promoted it. What does the Banting diet primarily restrict?
questions.explanation: The Banting diet is a low-carb, high-fat (LCHF) diet named after William Banting. Prof Tim Noakes sparked a national debate when he promoted it — even ending up in a medical hearing over it. South Africans either love it or love their pap too much to try it!

questions.question: Arrange these South African events from EARLIEST to LATEST: (1) First democratic election, (2) Discovery of gold on the Witwatersrand, (3) Sharpeville massacre, (4) Arrival of Jan van Riebeeck
options: Discovery of gold on the Witwatersrand
questions.explanation: Jan van Riebeeck arrived in 1652, gold discovered on the Witwatersrand in 1886, Sharpeville massacre in 1960, first democratic election in 1994. Only a true history buff gets this one right!
